title: Nonreplication of functional connectivity differences in autism spectrum disorder across multiple sites and denoising strategies
excerpt: A rapidly growing number of studies on autism spectrum disorder (ASD) have used resting-state fMRI to identify alterations of functional connectivity
citation: with the hope of identifying clinical biomarkers or underlying neural mechanisms. However
url_slug: results have been largely inconsistent across studies
paper_url: and there remains a pressing need to determine the primary factors influencing replicability. Here
slides_url: we used resting-state fMRI data from the Autism Brain Imaging Data Exchange to investigate two potential factors: denoising strategy and data site (which differ in terms of sample
column10: etc.). We examined the similarity of both group-averaged functional connectomes and group-level differences (ASD vs. control) across 33 denoising pipelines and four independently-acquired datasets. The group-averaged connectomes were highly consistent across pipelines (r = 0.92 ± 0.06) and sites (r = 0.88 ± 0.02). However
column12: while still consistent within site across pipelines (r = 0.76 ± 0.12)
column13: were highly inconsistent across sites regardless of choice of denoising strategies (r = 0.07 ± 0.04)
column14: suggesting lack of replication may be strongly influenced by site and/or cohort differences. Across-site similarity remained low even when considering the data at a large-scale network level or when considering only the most significant edges. We further show through an extensive literature survey that the parameters chosen in the current study (i.e.
column17: preprocessing methods) are quite representative of the published literature. These results highlight the importance of examining replicability in future studies of ASD
column20: call for extra caution when interpreting alterations in functional connectivity across groups of individuals.
